name: Dual-Process Reasoning (System 1 / System 2)
biological_basis: Cognition operates via two systems: System 1 (fast, automatic, associative, low effort) and System 2 (slow, deliberate, analytical, high effort). System 2 monitors and overrides System 1 when anomalies are detected.
core_claim: Most cognition is fast and automatic. Deliberate reasoning is triggered by surprise, conflict, or high stakes — engaging slower hypothesis testing.

implementable_mechanism.description: Fast path: prediction engine handles routine inputs when error < threshold. Slow path: when error > threshold, ReasoningLoop generates hypotheses, simulates outcomes, selects best explanation.
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: if prediction_error < threshold: return fast_predict(); else: hypotheses = generate(workspace, associations); return argmin_h simulate_error(h)

name: Structure-Mapping Analogy

source.author: Dedre Gentner
source.paper: Structure-mapping: A theoretical framework for analogy

biological_basis: Analogical reasoning maps relational structure from a familiar domain (base) to a novel domain (target). People prefer systematic, relation-rich mappings over superficial attribute matches.
core_claim: Analogy is structure mapping: align relational roles between domains, project inferences from base to target. Core of human abstract reasoning.

implementable_mechanism.description: Given active concepts, retrieve associated concepts via graph structure. Map relational patterns (co-activation paths) to generate candidate hypotheses. Evaluate by predicted consistency (error reduction).
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: associates = graph.neighbors(active); for a in associates: hypothesis = align_structure(active, a); score = predict_consistency(hypothesis); return best(hypotheses, by=score)

notes: ReasoningLoop uses association paths to generate and evaluate hypotheses.
